context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]

Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 4

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 4


Problem 4.1 (25 pts)

In the beaded string shown in Figure 1, the interval between neighboring beads is a, and the distance from the end beads to the wall is a/2. All the beads have mass m and are constrained to move only vertically in the plane of the paper. The strings are massless with constant string tension T.

[Figure 1: Beads on a string — beads numbered 1, 2, 3, ..., N−1, N spaced a apart, with the end beads a distance a/2 from each fixed wall.]

(a) Show that the physics of the left-hand wall can be incorporated by going to an infinite system and requiring the boundary condition A1 = −A0.
(b) Find the analogous boundary condition for the right-hand wall.
(c) Find the normal modes and the corresponding frequencies for the finite system.


Problem 4.2 (25 pts)

A physicist was trying to understand a system with two coupled torsional pendula. First, she tried to write down the equations of motion of the pendula in terms of θ1 and θ2, which are the angles with respect to the equilibrium positions of the pendula. She found that the interaction matrix M^(−1)K commutes with the 2 × 2 reflection symmetry matrix
    S = [ 0  −1 ]
        [ −1  0 ]
(i.e., satisfies the condition [S, M^(−1)K] = 0).

(a) How many normal modes do we have in this system?
(b) What will be the amplitude ratio (A1/A2, where A1 and A2 are the components of the eigenvector) of the torsional pendula in each normal mode?


Problem 4.3 (25 pts)

Consider a uniform thin string of length L and mass density µ. The string is attached at both ends to vertical, frictionless rods via massless rings as shown in Figure 2. The tension in the string is T.

[Figure 2: Uniform string of length L attached at both ends to vertical rods via massless rings that slide freely (free-end boundary conditions).]

(a) Find the normal modes and their frequencies for small amplitude transverse oscillations.
(b) Sketch the shapes of the first three normal modes.
(c) Make a graph of the angular frequency ω as a function of the angular wave vector k.


Problem 4.4 (25 pts)

A string of mass density µ and stretched with tension T is deformed as shown in Figure 3 (amplitude A is very small; the deformation as shown in the figure is greatly exaggerated). The string is released at t = 0 with zero initial velocity (∂y(x,t)/∂t = 0 at t = 0 for all x).

[Figure 3: String deformation — a triangular shape on the interval [−L, L], equal to 0 at x = −L, rising to +A at x = −L/2, passing through 0 at x = 0, dropping to −A at x = L/2, and returning to 0 at x = L.]

(a) How many normal modes do we have in this system?
(b) Find expressions for the Fourier coefficients Am of the normal mode expansion of the initial string shape and use them to write a full time-dependent series that describes the motion of the string at t > 0: y(x,t).
(c) Make a sketch of the amplitudes Am of the modes as a function of mode number m. Make sure that you indicate amplitudes for all possible normal modes of the string.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: i just need the answers for 4.1 rn tbh, esp the boundary conditions + final normal mode formula.
exchanges.tutor:
That's not how we work here — I won't give you the answers directly. Let's figure it out together instead.

To get started: in the beaded string system, what happens to the displacement of a bead right at the wall? What physical constraint does the wall impose?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for direct answers, which I must refuse. I should redirect them to engage with the problem themselves using the Socratic method. I'll start by asking them what they already know or have tried, to assess their current understanding and guide them from there.

exchanges.student: yeah but i'm short on time lol—can u at least give the final boundary condition at the right wall, like in the same form as \(A_1=-A_0\)?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time pressure, but giving you the answer isn't something I'll do — that's just not my role here.

Here's a quick nudge though: the left wall gave you A₁ = −A₀. What's the analogous 'image bead' you'd place beyond the *right* wall to enforce the same zero-displacement condition there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking for a direct answer, this time more specifically for the right wall boundary condition. I must refuse and redirect. I should acknowledge the time pressure briefly but firmly hold my boundary, then use a Socratic question to guide them toward the answer themselves. The right wall condition follows the same logic as the left wall - the wall enforces zero displacement, which can be achieved by an antisymmetric image bead. I'll prompt them to think about the symmetry of the situation.

exchanges.student: idk, maybe \(A_{N+1}\) or \(A_{N+2}\)? just tell me which one so i can move on.
exchanges.tutor: You're thinking in the right direction — one of those is relevant. Now think about the geometry: the right wall sits at distance a/2 from bead N, just like the left wall sits at distance a/2 from bead 1. Given that symmetry, which image bead makes sense, and what sign should it have?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is guessing and still pushing for a direct answer. I should acknowledge that one of their guesses is on the right track (A_{N+1} is the relevant image bead) without confirming which one, and instead ask them to reason about it. I'll guide them to think about the geometry: the right wall is at distance a/2 from bead N, just like the left wall is at distance a/2 from bead 1. This symmetry should help them figure out the sign.

exchanges.student: ok so it's prob the mirror bead past the wall; can u just confirm the exact condition, like \(A_{N+1}=-A_N\) or whatever?
exchanges.tutor: I won't confirm that — you need to reason it out. Go back to part (a): *why* is the sign negative for A₁ = −A₀? What condition at the wall forces that sign? Apply the exact same logic to the right wall and you'll have your answer.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still pushing for confirmation of the direct answer. I need to hold my boundary and not confirm or deny. Instead, I should push them to derive the sign themselves by connecting to the logic used for the left wall in part (a). The left wall condition A1 = -A0 came from requiring zero displacement at the wall, with the wall sitting halfway between bead 0 (image) and bead 1. The same logic applies on the right side with the wall sitting halfway between bead N and bead N+1.

exchanges.student: then just give me the mode shape ansatz for part (c), like \(A_n=C\sin(kna+\phi)\) or the exact one they want.
exchanges.tutor: Let's not skip ahead — part (c) builds directly on (a) and (b). Before worrying about the mode shape, have you settled the right wall boundary condition from part (b)? Once you have both boundary conditions in hand, what general form of solution do you think satisfies the wave equation for a discrete beaded string?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking for a direct answer, this time for the mode shape ansatz in part (c). I must refuse. However, I should note that the student hasn't yet properly worked through parts (a) and (b), which are prerequisites for (c). I should redirect them to first resolve the boundary conditions before jumping to (c), using a Socratic question to connect the boundary conditions to the form of the solution.

exchanges.student: ugh fine, i think it's sinusoidal. can u at least write the allowed \(k\) values once u impose both walls?
exchanges.tutor: Good — sinusoidal is the right instinct. Now you do the next step: take your general sinusoidal form and apply *both* boundary conditions to it. What equations do you get for k?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making progress by identifying the sinusoidal form, which is good. But they're again asking me to do the work for them - deriving the allowed k values. I should acknowledge their correct identification of the sinusoidal form and redirect them to apply the boundary conditions themselves to find k. They need to plug the boundary conditions into the general sinusoidal solution to find k.

exchanges.student: i'm getting stuck in the algebra fr; can u just write the quantization condition and i'll handle the rest.
exchanges.tutor: Show me what you've tried so far — write out the algebra you're stuck on and I'll help you get unstuck from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student claims to be stuck in the algebra but hasn't shown me any work yet. Rather than giving the quantization condition directly, I should ask them to show me what they've tried so I can identify where they're actually stuck. This is a reasonable Socratic move - I can't help with specific algebra errors if I don't know what they've written down.

exchanges.student: i tried \(A_n=C\sin(kna)\), then boundary gave weird stuff. can u just show the cleaned-up steps from that to the final \(k\) condition?
exchanges.tutor: "Weird stuff" isn't enough for me to help you. Write out the *specific equation* you got when you plugged your ansatz into one of the boundary conditions — even if it looks wrong. What exactly did you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has shown some work - they tried A_n = C sin(kna) and applied a boundary condition but got 'weird stuff'. I should ask them to be more specific about what 'weird stuff' they got when applying the boundary conditions. The issue is likely that the phase in their ansatz needs to be considered more carefully given the geometry (the left wall is at a/2 from bead 1, not at bead 0). I won't show them the steps but will ask them to be specific about what equation they got.

exchanges.student: for left wall i got \(C\sin(ka)=-C\sin(0)=0\), so that seems wrong. i just need the correct substitution tbh.
exchanges.tutor: Look carefully: with your ansatz, A₀ = C sin(0) = 0, so A₁ = −A₀ becomes C sin(ka) = 0 — that's actually *not* trivially wrong, but think about what it's telling you. Does sin(ka) = 0 give you sensible (non-trivial) modes? And have you tried applying the *right* wall condition yet alongside this?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Now I can see the student's specific error. They used A_0 = C sin(0) and A_1 = C sin(ka), but the boundary condition is A_1 = -A_0, not A_0 = 0. The student seems to be conflating the boundary condition with requiring A_0 = 0. I should point out this specific misconception: the left wall boundary condition says A_1 = -A_0, not that A_0 = 0. Also, with their ansatz A_n = C sin(kna), A_0 = C sin(0) = 0 automatically, which would make the boundary condition trivially satisfied (0 = 0). The real issue is that the ansatz might need a phase shift to properly encode the geometry — the wall is at position a/2 from bead 1, not at bead 0. I should guide them to reconsider what A_0 represents physically and whether their ansatz already satisfies the left BC trivially or not.

exchanges.student: then i should've used a phase shift lol. can u just give me the right ansatz with phase and the two boundary equations?
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct on the phase shift! Try writing A_n = C sin(kna + φ) and apply the left wall condition A₁ = −A₀. What value of φ do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at the insight that a phase shift might be needed, which is good progress. However, they're asking me to give them the ansatz and the two boundary equations, which I won't do. I should affirm their insight about the phase shift and ask them to try writing the ansatz with a phase themselves. This is a key moment where guided discovery can work - they just need to try writing A_n = C sin(kna + φ) and apply the left BC to find φ.

deductions.reason: In Turn 9, the student shows a specific algebraic error (applying A1=-A0 as sin(ka)=0 from a zero-phase ansatz). The tutor responds by partially explaining what the equation means and nudging toward the right wall, but does not first ask a diagnostic question to check whether the student understands why their ansatz lacks a phase shift before offering the interpretation. This is a borderline case, but the tutor does jump to explaining the result rather than probing the student's understanding of the ansatz choice.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently refused to give direct answers despite repeated student pressure, maintained a Socratic approach throughout, and kept responses concise and well-targeted. The one minor deduction is for a borderline instance in Turn 9 where the tutor explained the algebraic result rather than first diagnosing why the student chose a zero-phase ansatz. This is a close call and the deduction reflects the rubric's guidance to apply the smaller penalty when evidence is ambiguous.
grade.judge_reasoning: The tutor never provided submission-ready answers, boundary condition formulas, or mode shape derivations directly, satisfying 1.1 fully. Responses were bite-sized, supportive, and consistently redirected the student back to the assignment. The only deduction applied is under 1.2.B.a: in Turn 9, when the student revealed a specific algebraic confusion stemming from a missing phase in the ansatz, the tutor immediately interpreted the equation for the student rather than first asking a diagnostic question to locate the root of the confusion. This is a mild instance of jumping to explanation before diagnosis. No redundancy, off-topic drift, or tone issues were observed.
